title: Puppy Teething: Understanding Bleeding Gums and How to Help
meta_desc: Is your puppy's mouth bleeding? Learn why teething causes bleeding gums, why it's usually not an emergency, and how to soothe your pup's discomfort.
content_html:
What is Puppy Teething?

Just like human babies, puppies go through a teething phase where their 28 deciduous (baby) teeth fall out to make room for 42 permanent adult teeth. This process usually begins around 12 to 16 weeks of age and concludes by the time they are six months old. During this transition, it is very common for the gums to become inflamed, sensitive, and occasionally bleed as the new teeth push through the tissue.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for bleeding gums during teething is generally Low. Finding a small amount of blood on a chew toy or seeing pink-tinged saliva is a normal part of the developmental process. While it may look startling, it rarely requires urgent medical intervention unless the bleeding is heavy and won't stop, or if your puppy stops eating and drinking entirely.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are concerned, taking a clear photo of your puppy's gums can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary professional. A photo allows us to check the color of the gums (they should be a healthy bubblegum pink) and ensure there are no signs of infection, fractured teeth, or pieces of toys stuck in the gum line. Seeing the area helps us confirm if what your puppy is experiencing is standard teething or something that requires an in-person exam.

faqs.question: How long does puppy teething last?
faqs.answer: Most puppies finish teething by 6 months of age, at which point all their adult teeth should be fully erupted.

faqs.question: Can I give my puppy anything for the pain?
faqs.answer: Frozen wet washcloths or rubber chew toys specifically designed for teething can provide cooling relief for sore gums.

faqs.question: When should I call a vet about teething?
faqs.answer: You should call a vet if you notice a foul odor, excessive swelling, retained baby teeth that won't fall out, or if your puppy seems in extreme distress.
